skill.name: go-concurrency-patterns
skill.description: Guides Go developers through practical concurrency patterns using goroutines, channels, sync primitives, context cancellation, and race detection.
skill.summary: Build reliable concurrent Go applications with worker pools, pipelines, cancellation, synchronization, and testing guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported high-risk patterns, but review found they are false positives from Markdown examples and documentation text. No prompt injection, malicious intent, data exfiltration, or executable dangerous behavior was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Go Imports Misclassified as Dynamic Imports
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. These locations are Go import declarations inside Markdown code examples, not JavaScript dynamic import execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are within fenced Go examples and contain static import blocks. No executable skill script or dynamic loading behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The backticks are Markdown inline formatting and fenced code block delimiters for examples, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence appears in documentation files and surrounds Go or Bash examples. There is no script file, interpreter invocation, or user input execution path.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Misclassified as Network Activity
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The URLs point to public Go learning resources in a Resources section and are not fetched by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are Markdown links to official or educational documentation. No code sends data to those URLs or performs a network request.

low_findings.title: Ellipses Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged text is printed status output using an ellipsis, not a filesystem path or traversal sequence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are fmt.Printf examples that print worker status messages. They do not read, write, or construct filesystem paths.

low_findings.title: Channel Names Misclassified as C2 Indicators
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged c2 and c3 names are local Go channel variables in a fan-out and fan-in example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variables are adjacent to c1 in a concurrency example and are merged as channels. There is no command-and-control logic or external endpoint.

low_findings.title: Worker Examples Misclassified as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged worker functions demonstrate goroutine lifecycle handling and do not inspect the host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations define example worker functions that receive context and IDs. No system inventory commands, environment reads, or host probing are present.

low_findings.title: Skill Description Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The skill description discusses Go concurrency and contains no cryptographic algorithm or implementation guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is YAML front matter describing goroutines, channels, context, worker pools, and race conditions. No weak cryptographic primitive is referenced.

content.value_statement: Concurrent Go code is easy to leak, race, or block when patterns are unclear. This skill provides practical patterns for goroutines, channels, cancellation, worker pools, and race testing.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use goroutines, channels, mutexes, wait groups, and context.
Provides worker pool, fan-out and fan-in, semaphore, and graceful shutdown patterns.
Shows how to structure cancellation, deadlines, and error propagation in concurrent flows.
Guides use of race detection commands for tests, builds, and local runs.
Highlights common mistakes such as goroutine leaks and closing channels from receivers.
limitations: Focused on Go concurrency patterns, not general Go language training.
Does not run benchmarks or inspect live production systems by itself.
Examples are educational and may need adaptation for specific services.
Does not replace review of application-specific safety, timeout, and retry behavior.

use_cases.title: Design a Worker Pool
use_cases.description: Create a bounded worker model with cancellation, result collection, and clear shutdown behavior.

use_cases.title: Debug Race Conditions
use_cases.description: Review concurrent code paths and choose race detector commands and synchronization fixes.

use_cases.title: Plan Service Shutdown
use_cases.description: Add context cancellation and wait group coordination so workers exit cleanly during deployment or termination.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: I am building a Go feature with concurrent work. Help me choose between channels, a worker pool, a mutex, or errgroup.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Goroutines
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Go concurrency design for goroutine leaks, blocked channels, missing cancellation, and unsafe shared state.

prompt_templates.title: Add Graceful Shutdown
prompt_templates.prompt: Design graceful shutdown for this Go service using context cancellation, signal handling, wait groups, and a timeout.

prompt_templates.title: Tune a Concurrent Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Go pipeline and propose bounded concurrency, error propagation, backpressure, and race detection checks.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to process many jobs without starting unlimited goroutines.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a worker pool with a fixed worker count, a jobs channel, a results channel, and context cancellation.

output_examples.input: A team sees intermittent failures in a concurrent cache implementation.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests using the race detector, reviewing shared map access, and choosing sync.Map or a mutex-protected sharded map.

output_examples.input: A service needs to stop background workers during deployment.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines signal handling, context cancellation, wait group coordination, and a shutdown timeout.

best_practices: Start every concurrent design with ownership, cancellation, and shutdown rules.
Use bounded concurrency when work volume can grow beyond known limits.
Run the Go race detector before trusting shared state changes.
anti_patterns: Starting goroutines without a clear exit path or cancellation signal.
Closing channels from receivers or from multiple senders.
Using time.Sleep as a synchronization mechanism instead of proper coordination.

faq.question: Does this skill write production-ready Go code?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and examples that can guide production code. You should adapt and test them in your project.

faq.question: Can it help with goroutine leaks?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on context cancellation, channel closing rules, and wait group coordination.

faq.question: Does it cover mutexes as well as channels?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains channels, sync.Mutex, sync.RWMutex, sync.Map, wait groups, and related tradeoffs.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does the skill make network requests?
faq.answer: No. It includes documentation links and sample Go HTTP code, but the skill itself does not fetch external resources.

faq.question: Is this suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes. It starts with core primitives and also includes advanced patterns for experienced Go developers.
